question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to set out the supports available to students undertaking the Ulster University Social Work Honours degree course; and (ii) whether there are plans to increase the level of support, in light of the increased costs faced by the student population.
answer_text: My Department provides a Social Work Student Incentive Scheme for all NI Degree in Social Work students who are domicile and studying full-time in Northern Ireland. Social Work students can access a non-means tested payment of £4,000 for each year of their study.  In addition, they are awarded £500 per year to cover expenses whilst on Practice Learning placements. The DOH Student Incentive Scheme is in addition to any student finance support, which can be applied for separately.  I recognise the additional hardship faced by students undertaking clinical courses due to the cost of living crisis. Unfortunately, due to budgetary constraints, my Department is unable to provide more assistance to students than it does currently.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether (i) the issues affecting the NI Supply Teacher Register have been fully resolved;  and (ii) all supply teachers will receive payment in the next payroll.
answer_text: The Department funds the Education Authority to administer the NI Supply Teacher Register (NISTR) which is effectively a booking system enabling schools to source and engage professionally qualified teachers who have undergone a range of pre-employment checks.There have been a number of issues regarding the implementation of a new NISTR system and the timely payment of temporary teachers. The Department and the EA continue to work together intensively, alongside stakeholder groups, to ensure that immediate steps are taken to ensure staff are paid for their valuable service.  In the current exceptional circumstances, particularly in light of the cost-of-living crisis, the Department has asked the EA to process interim emergency payments to all teachers impacted as a matter of urgency.Due to administrative errors in extracting the file and uploading it to the payroll system; 309 temporary and supply teachers received only partial salary on 18 October for bookings that were approved by schools and should have been paid under normal circumstances.  The EA has sincerely apologised for this error and is processing interim emergency payments to reach these teachers by 25 October.The other factor that has caused problems in the timely payment of temporary teachers relates to the fact that the appropriate approval processes at school level were not completed in line with the payroll deadline.  This prevented payment being made on the planned date for an estimated 300 teachers.  Incorrect data input in respect of hours worked also appears to have featured in the sign-off process.  The EA is contacting all relevant schools to seek confirmation of attendance, check period of service and obtain other necessary sign-off information to enable further interim emergency payments to pursued.  In respect of the system issues, a number of fixes have been applied to address identified bugs and provide additional functionality for example, in line with strong school user feedback, a search by teacher name or TR number was added on 22 September.  Work continues to make further improvements and changes to system logic based on user feedback.  Planned additional features will continue to be added throughout the developmental phase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to set out the details of the £400 energy bill support scheme including (i) when it will be paid; and (ii) how it will be delivered to both those who pay by credit bill and by pay as you go meters.
answer_text: The United Kingdom Government (UKG) announced the £400 support in April and the Chancellor stated on the floor of the House in May that “we will legislate to deliver this support on the same terms in every part of the United Kingdom, including Northern Ireland.” I called for UKG to provide the support to local households as quickly as possible. 		UKG has suggested that the payment of £400 will be provided by electricity suppliers directly to electricity consumers in Northern Ireland in November 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether she will review the allowance paid to parents who receive a payment in lieu of school transport, in light of the increased cost of fuel.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for determining and administering parental payments and it has reviewed the scheme in light of the current financial climate and the increased pressures that the cost of fuel will have on parents in facilitating their child’s attendance at school. The EA has confirmed that all parental payments issued in the 2022/23 academic year will include a 5% uplift to the agreed standard payment values. This additional uplift has been agreed for the current academic year only and will be reviewed ahead of the 2023/24 academic year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline the criteria for providing a school bus to service roads in rural areas.
answer_text: My Department provides school transport assistance to all eligible pupils who meet the criteria laid out in transport circular 1996/41. These criteria apply to all pupils equally regardless of whether they live in a rural area or an urban area.Pupils must live at least 2 miles from their primary school, or 3 miles from their post primary school, and have formally sought and been unable to gain a place in any/all schools of the same category within the qualifying distance of their home. The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation and delivery of home to school transport which includes determining eligibility, the best method of delivering transport assistance for eligible pupils and route planning. Services provided and route planning are driven by demand from eligible pupils, whilst having due regard to the interests of efficiency and economy.   Given that they are more likely to live further from their school and therefore to be eligible for transport assistance, pupils from rural areas are well catered for under the policy. The EA makes every effort to provide a seat on a vehicle for eligible pupils either on EA or Translink services and plans routes accordingly. It is not obliged however to create, extend or alter routes to accommodate very small numbers or individual children, particularly where this would result in unreasonable public expenditure and in such circumstances, parental allowances are offered as an alternative to assist parents transporting their children to school.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how the capping of electricity and gas prices announced by the British government will be implemented for households and businesses here.
answer_text: I welcomed the announcement by the Business Secretary last week confirming the Energy Price Guarantee will be delivered by the Department for Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy (BEIS) in Northern Ireland. 		For households, the scheme will take effect from November with support being backdated to 1st October. This will ensure Northern Ireland households benefit from the same support as those in Great Britain with the support applying for 2 years. Similar support, for 6 months initially, for non-domestic consumers was also announced. 	BEIS is leading on the detailed delivery of both support schemes with the Northern Ireland Utility Regulator and local energy supply companies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what proposals his Department has put forward to support households that rely on home heating oil.
answer_text: On 21st September the UK Government (UKG) outlined new plans to help cut energy bills and gave further details on the support available for households in Great Britain and Northern Ireland. 	As part of this announcement, households who are connected to the electricity grid but who use fuels for heating other than natural gas, such as heating oil, will receive support through the Energy Price Guarantee for their electricity costs. The government has also announced that it will provide an additional payment of £100 to those who use home heating oil instead of gas. 		Officials from my Department alongside the Utility Regulator engaged with the UK Government throughout this process on the design and delivery of this payment to ensure its effectiveness for NI consumers. Furthermore, I have also encouraged UKG to reconsider this level of support for heating oil consumers given that over two-thirds of consumers here rely on the fuel for home heating and have provided information which demonstrates that more help is required.		It is vital that these support measures are rolled out as quickly as possible so that local business and domestic consumers are supported as their energy consumption rises into the winter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the replacement funding for programmes currently funded by the European Social Fund.
answer_text: The Department of Finance (DoF) leads the cross-departmental Future Policy & Finance working group and is engaged with the Department for Levelling Up, Housing and Communities (DLUHC) on funding streams associated with the Levelling Up agenda, including the Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF).We continue to work with DoF & DLUHC to try to ensure that replacement EU funding streams are directed to priority areas, in particular those aspects that fall within our policy remit, primarily young people not in education, employment and training (NEETs).As lead department, DoF officials continue work to gain a substantive role for the NI Executive in design and delivery of these funds. DLUHC has proposed establishment of a Partnership Group and in concert with local bodies will seek to develop and endorse an overarching NI Investment Plan for ultimate agreement by Whitehall Ministers.To date there has been very little information on roles, responsibilities, accountability or governance structures. It is expected that DLUHC will shortly start to reach out to NI organisations and bodies to participate on the Partnership Group.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how his Department is supporting businesses following the rise in energy costs and inflationary increases in input costs.
answer_text: Regulation exists for the largest electricity domestic supplier in Northern Ireland as there is insufficient competition in the market to ensure a fair deal for domestic consumers.  With a larger number of suppliers for business consumers, price regulation is driven by the market.  However, all consumers, including business customers, have the right to switch energy supplier and are protected in their dealings with energy companies through requirements in all energy supply licences.  In addition, Invest NI delivers a suite of support, including specialist advice and investment capital, to enable NI businesses to become more efficient and resilient through green efficiencies.  This includes fully funded Technical Consultancy, a Resource Matching Service, and the Resource Efficiency Capital Grant. This support helps businesses to optimise and futureproof their operations by implementing green efficiencies to achieve carbon savings and cost savings to mitigate the impact of rising energy costs.One of the key objectives of the ‘Path to Net Zero Energy’, the new Energy Strategy, published on 16th December 2021, is to ensure that all businesses have access to essential and affordable energy to enable an adequate standard of competitiveness. The focus is now on priorities for delivery to ensure that we maximise the benefits for our society and economy as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how the regulation of gas and electricity prices by the Utility Regulator compares to the price cap in Britain, including on the percentage profit that energy companies make.
answer_text: The current GB Price Cap, which was introduced on 1 April 2022, provides a combined electricity and gas price for the average household of £2,290 per year. Ofgem have recently announced that the GB Price Cap will increase to over £2,800 per year, from 1 October 2022. In Northern Ireland, from 1 July 2022, combined regulated electricity and gas prices in Belfast and the West Distribution area are £2,187 per year for the average household, and £2,448 per year in the Ten Towns Licence area. In Northern Ireland, the regulated profit margin for the gas supply companies is 2%, and Power NI has a profit margin of 2.2%. This compares to 3% within the Great Britain Price Cap.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on Action 21 of the The Path to Net Zero Energy Action Plan 2022.
answer_text: Action 21 requires a review of legislative and regulatory requirements in the context of energy decarbonisation goals. Work is continuing in collaboration with the Utility Regulator and other key stakeholders. This work is now also set in the context of the new Climate Change Act.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what his Department is doing to ensure energy generators and providers are not unfairly profiting from rising energy prices, while consumers struggle with bills.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland, the current system of pricing for consumers is a combination of price regulation and competitive pricing. Our price regulation of the dominant suppliers in electricity and gas ensures that customers pay no more than the efficient costs of purchasing and supplying gas and electricity plus an agreed profit margin set by the NI Utility Regulator (2% for gas and 2.2% for electricity). Reducing these caps (the minimum set by the Competitions and Market Authority would be 1.9%) would make almost no difference to the current price surges which are primarily due to wholesale cost increases driven by a rebound in fuel demand following the coronavirus pandemic and supply disruptions in the wake of Russia's invasion of Ukraine.  The regulated price then acts as a “price to beat” for the other suppliers.The Single Electricity Market Committee (SEMC), which is comprised of the Utility Regulator, the Irish Commission for Regulation of Utilities, and two independent members, regulates the Single Electricity Market (SEM), in which generation is traded across the island of Ireland.  Through the Energy Trading Arrangements (ETA) within the SEM, generators compete to sell electricity. A number of markets each spanning different trading timeframes (Forward, Day Ahead, Intraday and the Balancing markets) have been designed within SEM to enable increasing levels of competition that place a downward pressure on prices whilst ensuring that the supply of power matches demand.  The unabated rises in energy bills, driven by our continued reliance on importing fossil fuels, highlights the importance of our local Energy Strategy ‘The Path to Net Zero Energy’ which aims to reduce the energy we use by investing in energy efficiency and take advantage of our natural resources to generate clean indigenous energy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 30705/17-22,  whether he will give further consideration to (i) emergency powers to protect consumers in light of ongoing high oil prices; and (ii) the need for regulation of off-grid energy, including home heating oil, as reflected in the views of the Consumer Council and the Competition and Markets Authority.
answer_text: The ongoing energy crisis continues to have a serious impact on the cost of all fossil fuel energy sources and despite the existence of a competitive local home heating oil supply market, external factors have unfortunately resulted in a further increase in global oil prices, which are passed on to consumers. It is important to draw a distinction between price regulation and non-price regulation. In a report by the Office of Fair Trading (OFT) in 2011, it was highlighted that regulation was a solution where markets cannot be expected to work well of their own accord, for example where natural monopolies arise, which is the case for gas and electricity. The same report highlights that it is only the transmission and distribution networks for electricity and gas which are regulated, with the commodity price set by the market. This is clearly evidenced by the substantial increase in natural gas and electricity prices over the last year. The OFT report also stated that “regulation would be unlikely to have a significant impact on overall prices [for oil] – probably no more than is currently achievable by shopping around” as a price regulator would have to allow legitimate pass-through costs, such as the crude oil price. In addition, there are likely to be costs associated with regulation which would be passed on to consumers.  My Department has no immediate plans to seek emergency powers to introduce regulation for the heating oil sector at this time, however it will keep this under review. My Department continues to recognise the importance of consumer protection and are engaging with relevant stakeholders, such as the Consumer Council. The Consumer Council Heating Oil Price Checker provides consumers with information on the average price of heating oil, based on weekly information collated from around 92 oil suppliers, with efforts made to ensure a geographical spread of price data from all 11 Council areas.The Competition and Markets Authority has jurisdiction to investigate and take action to address anti-competitive practices. My Department’s Trading Standards Services (TSS) is the primary weights and measures authority in Northern Ireland and enforces the legislation controlling the sale and delivery of home heating oil. TSS investigated a high volume of complaints in March 2022, where complainants alleged they had been quoted a price, made payment on the day of the order, but were subsequently informed that the price had increased on the day of delivery. In response to these complaints TSS issued a statement through the media, wrote to several home heating oil suppliers, and met with a number of traders and the Northern Ireland Oil Federation to provide advice on how to comply with contract and consumer law. Given the unprecedented rise in energy costs, TSS will also soon commence a province-wide exercise to ensure that road tanker measuring equipment within the home heating oil sector is legally compliant and consumers are getting the quantit

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the Shared Prosperity Fund, including how it will be administered and when it will open for applications.
answer_text: The Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF) prospectus was published on the 14th April and lacked detail including how it will be administered here or when it will open for applications. DLUHC are proposing to convene a group here to develop an investment plan for SPF over the summer. It is anticipated that this group is to be chaired by DLUHC with membership drawn from various sectors. Engaging in these structures in the absence of a clear Executive mandate raises legal issues and I remain deeply concerned at disregard for our devolved competence. I am currently engaging with DLUHC on this issue but as yet no agreement on our role within this structure has been reached. The DHLUC approach means that decisions on spending in devolved areas will ultimately rest with British Government Ministers. This outcome does not address the concerns raised both by my officials and by the Equality Commission in their recent report on the replacement of EU funding.It is important that any funding delivered here aligns to our strategic objectives, is complementary to other funding streams and provides value for money.  I will continue to engage with DLUHC to seek an active and substantial role in the development and delivery of this fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) what support is available from his Department for rural community groups; and (ii) whether there are plans to review the availability of resource funding to such groups.
answer_text: DAERA, through the Tackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation (TRPSI) provides a range of revenue and capital support programmes for rural community groups including:		ProgrammeAnnual amount £Local Rural Community Support Service Programme 917,873Regional support through DfC Regional Infrastructure Support Programme. 211,000Assisted Rural Travel Scheme 700,000Rural Support Charity and Social Farming Support Service307,627Rural Micro Capital Grants Scheme 800,000								In addition a number of Pilot Projects have been undertaken to help inform the future Rural Business and Communities Investment Fund which is currently being developed as a successor to the Leader Rural Development Programme under the Rural Policy Framework. These include the Rural Halls Refurbishment Programme which provides capital funding for the refurbishment of halls together with a capacity building element to help increase the future sustainability of these groups. This Programme was allocated £4.5m for the 3 year period 2021 - 2023.		Future funding requirements for rural community groups will be considered within the context of the new Rural Policy Framework.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what the current average waiting time is for a first appointment with the regional plastic surgery team in the Ulster Hospital.
answer_text: The current average waiting time for a first appointment with the regional plastic surgery team in the Ulster Hospital is 94.7 weeks.  This reflects how long on average patients had waited up until 31st March 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what support is available from her Department for rural community groups.
answer_text: The Department’s Regional Infrastructure Support Programme provides for regional level support that voluntary and community sector organisations need in order to function effectively and efficiently.  My Department has a funding partnership with DAERA and together we fund a consortium of partners including the Rural Community Network and the Rural Women’s Network to deliver this programme.  In additional to general support, the Programme provides a specific element of support for women’s organisations in rural and disadvantaged areas, and for volunteering and advice services.  My Department also supports local community development infrastructure in rural and urban areas through the Community Investment Fund and contributes to local government delivery of community development and advice services across all Council areas through its Community Support Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the Special Recognition Payment will be made to healthcare workers who have moved employer; and whether the payment will be made by the former employer.
answer_text: Phase 2, which is concerned with healthcare workers who have moved employer, had been temporarily paused to allow officials to seek clarity on a number of issues raised relating to the impact of the increase in national insurance contributions, which are included as part of the payment provided to the former employer.  A further letter was issued to employers on 14 April 2022 providing clarity on the issues raised and confirming recommencement of the payment process. Claims are being processed by BSO in date order when the claimants provide all relevant information. No date has been set for the closure of the scheme and claims will continue to be processed by the Business Services Organisation during 2022/23. I am not in a position to provide a date as to when all payments will be made.I can confirm that as at 26 May 2022, a total of over £16.6m has been paid out under Phase 1 to independent sector care home and domiciliary care providers covering 28,457 individuals. As at the same date over £3.3m has been paid out to 7,999 individuals under Phase 2; these payments include staff previously employed in independent sector care home and domiciliary care providers who have since moved employer or left the sector, and staff employed with eligible day opportunity providers and buildings-based day services.In addition we have also opened the Scheme to eligible applicants in the following groups, Personal Assistants employed by Independent Living Fund recipients, Thalidomide Health Grant recipients, and Personal Assistants employed by those in receipt of Direct Payments from HSC Trusts. This element of the scheme was launched on 23 March and is being delivered by ILF Scotland for the payments, Scotland Excel who set up and run the online portal, and SDS Scotland who manage the dedicated helpline. These delivery partners were heavily involved in delivering the equivalent scheme for the Scottish Government.  To date £300k has been paid to 863 individuals. To encourage uptake on this element Trusts have now written to all individuals in receipt of direct payments. My officials are also finalising arrangements to include eligible staff whose employers are no longer trading and I hope to be in a position to include this cohort of individuals in the coming weeks.A FAQ on the Special Recognition Payment has been published on the Department’s website as below:-https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/news/hsc-staff-recognition-payment-faqsThis continues to be updated with relevant information as each phase of the claims process progresses. I remain personally committed to ensuring the recognition payment will be paid to independent sector staff as soon as it is practicable.
